subject: Tort Law
lesson_title: Elements of Defamation, Defences, and the Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024

questions.question: What are the three elements a plaintiff must prove to establish defamation under the Defamation Act 2009?

options.A: That the defendant made a statement; that the statement was defamatory; and that the statement was published to at least one person other than the plaintiff
options.B: That the defendant acted with malice; that the statement caused actual damage; and that the statement was published in a permanent form
options.C: That the statement was false; that it caused reputational damage; and that the defendant failed to take reasonable care before publishing
options.D: That the defendant identified the plaintiff; that the statement was widely circulated; and that the plaintiff's reputation was irreparably harmed

questions.explanation: Under the Defamation Act 2009, the three elements are: (1) the defendant made a statement; (2) the statement was defamatory — that is, it tends to injure the plaintiff's reputation in the eyes of reasonable members of society; and (3) the statement was published to at least one person other than the plaintiff. Malice and actual damage are not general requirements.

questions.question: The Defamation Act 2009 replaced which earlier Irish legislation governing defamation law?

options.A: The Civil Liability Act 1961
options.B: The Defamation Act 1961
options.C: The Newspaper Libel and Registration Act 1881
options.D: The Broadcasting Act 2009

questions.explanation: The Defamation Act 2009 replaced the Defamation Act 1961 and significantly modernised Irish defamation law. The 2009 Act was itself subsequently amended by the Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024, which introduced further major reforms including the abolition of jury trials in High Court defamation proceedings.

questions.question: Under section 2 of the Defamation Act 2009, which of the following falls within the definition of a 'statement' for the purposes of defamation law?

options.A: Only written or printed publications in permanent form
options.B: Only broadcast statements made on television or radio
options.C: Words, pictures, visual images, gestures, or any other method of signifying meaning, whether oral, written, broadcast, or otherwise
options.D: Only statements made by or attributable to an identifiable individual defendant

questions.explanation: Section 2 of the Defamation Act 2009 defines 'statement' broadly to encompass words, pictures, visual images, gestures, or other methods of signifying meaning — whether made orally, in writing, in broadcast form, or in any other manner. This broad definition encompasses social media posts, tweets, videos, and online comments as well as traditional print and broadcast media.

questions.question: Under the Defamation Act 2009, the test for whether a statement is defamatory is:

options.A: Subjective — whether the plaintiff personally understood the statement as damaging to their reputation
options.B: Determined by expert witnesses providing evidence of the reputational effect in the relevant field
options.C: Based on whether the defendant intended to lower the plaintiff's reputation in the eyes of others
options.D: Objective — whether the statement tends to injure the plaintiff's reputation in the eyes of reasonable members of society

questions.explanation: The test for whether a statement is defamatory is objective. The question is how reasonable members of society would understand the statement, not how the plaintiff personally understood it or what the defendant intended. This objective standard was confirmed by Walsh J in Quigley v Creation Limited [1971], where the test was stated as whether the statement would tend to lower the plaintiff in the estimation of right-thinking members of society.

questions.question: What did the Irish Supreme Court confirm in Quigley v Creation Limited [1971] regarding the test for defamation?

options.A: That the relevant test is whether the statement would tend to lower the plaintiff in the estimation of right-thinking members of society, applying an objective standard
options.B: That malice is a required element for all categories of defamatory statement under Irish law
options.C: That only written statements in permanent form can be defamatory under Irish law
options.D: That the plaintiff must prove actual reputational damage before a defamation action can succeed

questions.explanation: In Quigley v Creation Limited [1971], Walsh J confirmed the objective standard for defamatory meaning in Irish law: the relevant test is whether the statement would tend to lower the plaintiff in the estimation of right-thinking members of society. It is the reaction of the reasonable person, not the plaintiff's subjective view, that determines whether a statement is defamatory.

questions.question: In the law of defamation, what is meant by a 'defamatory innuendo'?

options.A: A statement that is plainly and obviously defamatory on its face without any need for additional context
options.B: A secondary meaning that is defamatory only when the statement is read in the context of additional information known to the recipient, and which must be specifically pleaded
options.C: A false statement made for the purpose of injuring the plaintiff's trade or profession rather than their general reputation
options.D: A statement made in circumstances where the defendant clearly intended the words to be understood in a defamatory sense

questions.explanation: A defamatory innuendo is a secondary meaning that is defamatory only when the statement is read in the context of additional information known to the recipient. Unlike a statement that is defamatory in its natural and ordinary meaning, innuendo depends on the recipient's knowledge of particular facts. The Court of Appeal in Nolan v Sunday Newspapers Ltd [2019] confirmed that innuendo must be specifically pleaded and the plaintiff must identify the secondary meaning relied upon.

questions.question: Sinclair v Gogarty [1937] is cited as a classic Irish illustration of which concept in defamation law?

options.A: Absolute privilege attaching to statements made during court proceedings
options.B: The publication requirement — that communication to the plaintiff alone does not found a defamation action
options.C: Defamatory innuendo — where an apparently neutral statement becomes defamatory by reason of additional facts known to the recipient
options.D: Qualified privilege and the duty-interest test applied to personal communications

questions.explanation: Sinclair v Gogarty [1937] is the classic Irish authority illustrating defamatory innuendo. In that case, a statement that appeared neutral on its face became defamatory by innuendo when the relationship between the persons described and the background circumstances were known to the reader. The case demonstrates that a statement may be defamatory not in its natural meaning but by virtue of additional facts known to the recipient.

questions.question: What does the 'publication' element of defamation require?

options.A: That the statement was published in a newspaper, magazine, or other recognised media outlet with general circulation
options.B: That the statement was made available to the general public rather than to a limited or private audience
options.C: That the statement was communicated in writing, since oral statements cannot give rise to a defamation action
options.D: That the statement was communicated to at least one person other than the plaintiff

questions.explanation: The publication element of defamation requires that the statement was communicated to at least one person other than the plaintiff. An email opened only by the plaintiff, or words spoken only to the plaintiff in private, do not satisfy the publication requirement. There is no requirement that the publication reach a wide audience — communication to a single third party is sufficient.

questions.question: What did the Court of Appeal confirm in Nolan v Sunday Newspapers Ltd [2019] regarding claims of defamatory innuendo?

options.A: That innuendo must be specifically pleaded and the plaintiff must identify the secondary meaning being relied upon
options.B: That innuendo claims can only succeed where the defendant knew of the additional facts that made the statement defamatory
options.C: That a news article whose surface meaning is entirely neutral cannot give rise to an innuendo claim regardless of context
options.D: That the court must instruct the jury on the innuendo meaning before any other question is considered at trial

questions.explanation: In Nolan v Sunday Newspapers Ltd [2019], the Court of Appeal confirmed that innuendo must be specifically pleaded in defamation proceedings and that the plaintiff must identify the secondary meaning being relied upon. The court considered a claim of innuendo arising from a news article whose surface meaning was neutral but whose underlying implication was defamatory when read in context of information known to readers.

questions.question: Under section 16 of the Defamation Act 2009, what is required for a defendant to succeed on the truth defence?

options.A: That the defendant can prove every word of the statement is literally and completely accurate in all its particulars
options.B: That the defendant can prove the statement is substantially true — the gist of the statement is true even if minor details are inaccurate
options.C: That the plaintiff is unable to demonstrate any particular damage to their reputation from the publication
options.D: That the defendant published the statement in good faith with an honest belief in its truth at the time of publication

questions.explanation: Under section 16 of the Defamation Act 2009, the truth defence requires the defendant to prove that the statement is substantially true. The gist of the statement must be true, but if minor or peripheral details are inaccurate, the defence may still succeed. The burden of proving truth rests on the defendant. Good faith belief in truth, without proof of actual truth, is not sufficient.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes absolute privilege under section 17 of the Defamation Act 2009?

options.A: A privilege available to media publications on matters of public interest where the defendant acted fairly and responsibly
options.B: A privilege protecting statements made in good faith, provided the defendant did not act with malice toward the plaintiff
options.C: A complete and unconditional protection from defamation liability, regardless of the truth or falsity of the statement or any malice on the part of the defendant
options.D: A privilege applying to statements made in the course of employment, where the employer had a legitimate interest in receiving the information

questions.explanation: Absolute privilege under section 17 of the Defamation Act 2009 provides a complete and unconditional protection from defamation liability — regardless of the truth or falsity of the statement and regardless of any malice on the part of the defendant. It cannot be defeated by proving malice, unlike qualified privilege. It applies to statements in Oireachtas proceedings and judicial proceedings, among other specified contexts.

questions.question: Statements made during debates and proceedings of the Oireachtas attract absolute privilege in Irish law. What is the constitutional basis for this protection?

options.A: Section 17 of the Defamation Act 2009, which codifies all categories of absolute privilege in a single statutory provision
options.B: Article 40.6.1 of the Constitution, which guarantees freedom of expression and encompasses freedom of political speech
options.C: The common law doctrine of parliamentary privilege, adopted wholesale into Irish law upon independence
options.D: Article 15.12 of the Constitution, which provides that members shall not be questioned in any court in respect of utterances in either House

questions.explanation: The constitutional basis for absolute privilege for Oireachtas proceedings is Article 15.12 of the Constitution, which provides that members of either House shall not be questioned in any court or any authority other than the House itself in respect of any utterance in either House. This provides complete constitutional immunity for statements made during Oireachtas debates and proceedings.

questions.question: What did the Supreme Court consider in Higgins v Irish Aviation Authority [2022] in the context of absolute privilege?

options.A: The extent to which absolute privilege applies to statements made in the context of statutory inquiries and quasi-judicial proceedings
options.B: Whether social media posts by public officials attract absolute privilege by virtue of their office
options.C: The relationship between absolute privilege and the right to reputation protected by Article 40.3.2 of the Constitution
options.D: Whether the Defamation Act 2009 entirely replaced the common law rules on absolute privilege

questions.explanation: In Higgins v Irish Aviation Authority [2022], the Supreme Court considered the extent to which absolute privilege applies to statements made in the context of statutory inquiries and quasi-judicial proceedings. The Court confirmed a principled approach to identifying when absolute privilege attaches beyond the core contexts of Oireachtas proceedings and judicial proceedings, examining what features of a proceeding are required to attract the privilege.

questions.question: What is the classic test for qualified privilege in the law of defamation?

options.A: That the defendant made the statement in good faith without knowledge of its falsity and without any malicious motive
options.B: That the person making the statement had a legal, moral, or social duty to make it, and the recipient had a corresponding interest in receiving it
options.C: That the statement was made in circumstances where the defendant reasonably believed it to be true and of public benefit
options.D: That the defendant is a media organisation publishing on a matter of public interest in accordance with fair journalistic standards

questions.explanation: The classic test for qualified privilege is the duty-interest test: qualified privilege applies where the person making the statement had a legal, moral, or social duty to make it, and the recipient had a corresponding interest in receiving it. Employment references are a paradigm example — the employer has a social duty to provide an honest reference and the prospective employer has an interest in receiving it. Qualified privilege can be defeated by proof of malice.

questions.question: How may the defence of qualified privilege be defeated in a defamation action?

options.A: By showing that the statement was published to a wider audience than was necessary for the privileged purpose
options.B: By demonstrating that the statement was incorrect in any material particular, however minor
options.C: By proving that the defendant was actuated by malice — that they used the privileged occasion for a purpose other than that for which the privilege was granted
options.D: By establishing that the defendant had no reasonable basis for believing the statement to be true at the time of publication

questions.explanation: Qualified privilege is defeated by proving malice — that the defendant was actuated by an improper motive and used the privileged occasion for some purpose other than the purpose for which the privilege was granted. Mere inaccuracy does not defeat qualified privilege; what is required is proof of improper motive. Excess of publication may go to the question of whether the occasion was in fact privileged.

questions.question: Under section 20 of the Defamation Act 2009, for the honest opinion defence to apply, what must the statement indicate?

options.A: That the defendant conducted thorough research before expressing the opinion
options.B: That the opinion has a factual basis in documents already available to the public
options.C: That the matter on which the opinion is expressed is of significant public concern
options.D: That the statement is an expression of opinion, either expressly or by implication

questions.explanation: The first requirement for the honest opinion defence under section 20 of the Defamation Act 2009 is that the statement must indicate — expressly or by implication — that it is an expression of opinion rather than a statement of fact. If the statement is presented as a factual assertion rather than an opinion, the defence cannot apply. The remaining requirements are that the opinion is honestly held, based on true facts or privileged material, and relates to a matter of public interest.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly lists all four requirements for the honest opinion defence under section 20 of the Defamation Act 2009?

options.A: The statement indicates it is an opinion; the opinion is honestly held; it is based on facts that are true or substantially true or on privileged material; and it relates to a matter of public interest
options.B: The statement is an expression of opinion; it was published by a media defendant; the relevant facts were independently verified; and no offer of amends was made prior to proceedings
options.C: The statement indicates an opinion; the opinion is based on true facts; the defendant had no malicious intent; and the opinion relates to the conduct of a public figure
options.D: The opinion was expressed without malice; the defendant had a duty to express it; the facts on which it was based were correct; and it was published by a qualified media organisation

questions.explanation: The four requirements for the honest opinion defence under section 20 of the Defamation Act 2009 are: (1) the statement indicates, expressly or by implication, that it is an opinion; (2) the opinion is honestly held; (3) the opinion is based on facts that are true or substantially true, or on privileged material; and (4) the opinion relates to a matter of public interest. There is no requirement that the defendant be a media organisation.

questions.question: Under section 22 of the Defamation Act 2009, what is the effect when the plaintiff unreasonably refuses an offer of amends made by the defendant?

options.A: The plaintiff's claim is automatically struck out and no further proceedings may be brought in respect of the same publication
options.B: The defendant may rely on the plaintiff's unreasonable refusal of the offer of amends as a defence to the defamation proceedings
options.C: The plaintiff is required to pay the defendant's legal costs incurred from the date of the unreasonable refusal onwards
options.D: The court will assess damages on the basis that no actionable defamation occurred, treating the offer as curing any reputational harm

questions.explanation: Under section 22 of the Defamation Act 2009, an offer of amends allows a defendant who has made an unintentional defamatory statement to offer a suitable correction, apology, and compensation. If the plaintiff unreasonably refuses such an offer, the defendant may rely on that refusal as a defence to the defamation proceedings. The offer of amends defence creates an incentive for plaintiffs to accept reasonable settlement proposals.

questions.question: The defence of fair and reasonable publication under section 26 of the Defamation Act 2009 is primarily available to which category of defendant?

options.A: Any person making a statement in a professional or quasi-professional capacity where there is a public interest in the subject matter
options.B: Individual members of the public commenting on matters of local or community concern
options.C: Media defendants publishing statements on matters of public interest
options.D: Any defendant who can demonstrate they conducted adequate research and verification before making the statement

questions.explanation: The fair and reasonable publication defence under section 26 of the Defamation Act 2009 is available to media defendants publishing statements on matters of public interest. Section 26(2) sets out eight factors the court must consider in assessing whether the publication was fair and reasonable, including the public benefit of the statement, seriousness of the allegation, whether the plaintiff was sought for comment, and the extent of the defendant's verification of facts.

questions.question: In Kinsella v Kenmare Resources plc [2019], which defence did the High Court apply when considering whether publication of a serious allegation in a corporate context was protected?

options.A: The truth defence under section 16, on the basis that the serious allegation was shown to be substantially accurate
options.B: Qualified privilege under section 18, applying the duty-interest test to the corporate communications context
options.C: Honest opinion under section 20, finding the serious allegation to be an expression of opinion in a matter of public interest
options.D: Fair and reasonable publication under section 26, examining whether the defendant's publication met the required standard of responsible journalism on a matter of public interest

questions.explanation: In Kinsella v Kenmare Resources plc [2019], the High Court applied the fair and reasonable publication defence under section 26 of the Defamation Act 2009, considering whether the defendant's publication of a serious allegation in a corporate context met the standard of fair and reasonable publication on a matter of public interest. The case is an important application of the section 26 test to allegations in a non-traditional media context.

questions.question: The innocent publication defence under section 27 of the Defamation Act 2009 primarily protects which category of defendant?

options.A: Distributors, printers, and other secondary publishers who had no knowledge or reason to suspect that the material they disseminated contained defamatory content
options.B: Journalists who published statements in good faith relying on information from a confidential source
options.C: Defendants who made the defamatory statement in a personal capacity rather than in the course of a business or trade
options.D: Internet platforms that removed defamatory content promptly upon receiving a formal complaint from the plaintiff

questions.explanation: The innocent publication defence under section 27 of the Defamation Act 2009 applies to distributors, printers, and other secondary publishers who had no knowledge, and no reason to suspect, that the material they were disseminating contained defamatory content. This defence recognises that secondary publishers — who did not create the content — should not be liable in defamation where they were genuinely unaware of its defamatory character.

questions.question: Which of the following is described as the most dramatic change introduced by the Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024?

options.A: The introduction of a statutory single publication rule for all categories of defamation proceedings
options.B: The abolition of the right to trial by jury in defamation proceedings in the High Court
options.C: The creation of a new statutory defence of responsible publication for individual social media users
options.D: The extension of the general limitation period for defamation from one year to two years

questions.explanation: The most dramatic change introduced by the Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024 was the abolition of the right to trial by jury in defamation proceedings in the High Court. Prior to the 2024 Act, parties in High Court defamation proceedings had a right to jury trial — a distinctive feature of Irish defamation law that contributed to high costs and unpredictability. Defamation cases in the High Court are now tried by a judge alone.

questions.question: What was the position regarding jury trials in High Court defamation proceedings immediately prior to the Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024?

options.A: Jury trials in defamation cases had already been abolished by a Supreme Court ruling before the 2024 Act was enacted
options.B: Jury trials were available only at the discretion of the trial judge and were rarely granted in practice
options.C: Parties in High Court defamation proceedings had a right to trial by jury — a distinctive feature of Irish defamation law
options.D: Jury trials were available only for defamation claims in which damages exceeded a specified statutory financial threshold

questions.explanation: Immediately prior to the Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024, parties in High Court defamation proceedings had a right to trial by jury. This was a distinctive feature of Irish defamation law that distinguished it from many other civil proceedings. The right to jury trial contributed to the high costs and unpredictability associated with defamation litigation, which was a significant driver of the 2024 reform.

questions.question: What does the acronym 'SLAPP' stand for in the context of the Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024?

options.A: Serious Legal Actions Against Public Publishers
options.B: Strategic Litigation Affecting Press Proceedings
options.C: Statutory Legal Actions for the Prevention of Publicity
options.D: Strategic Litigation Against Public Participation

questions.explanation: SLAPP stands for Strategic Litigation Against Public Participation. It refers to defamation or other legal claims brought not to vindicate genuine reputational harm but to silence critics, activists, journalists, or civil society organisations through the chilling effect of expensive litigation. The Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024 introduced a fast-track mechanism for defendants to have SLAPP proceedings dismissed at an early stage.

questions.question: What anti-SLAPP mechanism did the Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024 introduce into Irish law?

options.A: A fast-track procedure for defendants to apply to have SLAPP proceedings dismissed at an early stage, before the full costs of litigation are incurred
options.B: A statutory cap on damages that can be awarded in proceedings identified by the court as SLAPP actions
options.C: A criminal offence for parties who bring defamation proceedings with the primary purpose of silencing public participation
options.D: A mandatory mediation process for all defamation claims involving media defendants or matters of public interest

questions.explanation: The Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024 introduced a fast-track mechanism for defendants to apply to have SLAPP proceedings dismissed at an early stage — before the full costs of defending a defamation action are incurred. This procedural safeguard is designed to prevent the chilling effect of strategic litigation on legitimate public participation, journalism, and civil society activity. The mechanism aligns with the EU Anti-SLAPP Directive.

questions.question: With which EU instrument does the anti-SLAPP fast-track mechanism in the Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024 align?

options.A: The EU Whistleblower Protection Directive (2019/1937/EU), which protects persons reporting breaches of EU law
options.B: The EU Anti-SLAPP Directive (2024/1069/EU), which requires member states to provide procedural safeguards against SLAPP actions in matters with cross-border implications
options.C: The EU Digital Services Act, which addresses platform liability and content moderation obligations for online intermediaries
options.D: The EU Charter of Fundamental Rights, Articles 11 and 47, on freedom of expression and effective access to justice

questions.explanation: The anti-SLAPP fast-track mechanism in the Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024 aligns with the EU Anti-SLAPP Directive (Directive 2024/1069/EU), which requires member states to introduce procedural safeguards against SLAPP actions in matters with cross-border implications. The Directive aims to protect journalists, human rights defenders, and civil society organisations from abusive litigation used to silence public participation.

questions.question: What change did the Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024 make to the limitation period for online defamation?

options.A: It extended the limitation period for online defamation from one year to three years from the date of first online publication
options.B: It confirmed the multiple publication rule, providing that each re-publication online triggers a fresh one-year limitation period
options.C: It moved towards a single publication rule, limiting the limitation period to one year from the date of first online publication
options.D: It abolished the limitation period entirely for defamatory content published anonymously on social media platforms

questions.explanation: The Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024 moved towards a single publication rule for online defamation, limiting the limitation period to one year from the date of first online publication. This addressed the problem created by the multiple publication rule — under which each re-publication of a defamatory statement online could trigger a fresh cause of action and a fresh limitation period — which exposed defendants to potentially indefinite liability for internet content.

questions.question: What problem did the 'multiple publication rule' create for online defamation defendants prior to the 2024 Act reforms?

options.A: It prevented defendants from relying on the truth defence for statements that had been re-published by independent third parties
options.B: It required defendants to issue separate apologies on each platform on which a defamatory statement had been published
options.C: It meant that plaintiffs could bring claims against both the original publisher and every person who shared or re-tweeted the statement
options.D: Each re-publication of a defamatory statement online could trigger a fresh cause of action and a fresh limitation period, creating potentially indefinite exposure for defendants

questions.explanation: The multiple publication rule — as applied to online defamation before the 2024 Act — meant that each re-publication, re-tweet, or sharing of a defamatory statement online constituted a fresh publication, potentially triggering a fresh cause of action and a fresh limitation period. This exposed defendants to indefinite liability for material that remained accessible on the internet for years or decades after the original publication, which the 2024 Act addressed by moving towards a single publication rule.

questions.question: What does the Norwich Pharmacal jurisdiction allow a defamation plaintiff to do?

options.A: Apply to court for an order compelling a third party such as a social media platform or internet service provider to disclose the identity of an anonymous defamer
options.B: Apply to court for an interim injunction preventing publication of an allegedly defamatory statement before trial
options.C: Compel the defendant to disclose the journalistic sources used in preparing a defamatory publication
options.D: Require an online platform to remove all copies of a defamatory statement within 24 hours of receiving a court order

questions.explanation: The Norwich Pharmacal jurisdiction — derived from Norwich Pharmacal Co v Customs and Excise Commissioners and applied in Ireland in Muwema v Facebook Ireland Ltd [2016] — allows a plaintiff to apply to court for an order compelling a third party (such as a social media platform or internet service provider) to disclose the identity of an anonymous defamer. The Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024 streamlined and clarified this procedure for the online context.

questions.question: What did the Irish High Court address in Muwema v Facebook Ireland Ltd [2016]?

options.A: Whether Facebook could be held directly liable as a primary publisher for defamatory content posted by its users after notification
options.B: The application of the Norwich Pharmacal jurisdiction to compel Facebook to disclose the identity of an anonymous online defamer
options.C: Whether the single publication rule applied to defamatory posts that had been shared multiple times on the Facebook platform
options.D: Whether the Defamation Act 2009 applied to social media platforms incorporated in Ireland but primarily serving users in other jurisdictions

questions.explanation: In Muwema v Facebook Ireland Ltd [2016], the Irish High Court addressed the application of the Norwich Pharmacal jurisdiction to compel Facebook to disclose the identity of an anonymous online defamer. The case is an important Irish authority on the use of this jurisdiction in the online context, and the approach was subsequently reinforced and streamlined by the Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024.

questions.question: When considering the liability of online platforms for defamatory content posted by their users, what EU instrument must be read alongside the Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024?

options.A: The EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), which governs the processing of personal data by online platforms and intermediaries
options.B: The EU Audiovisual Media Services Directive, which governs editorial responsibility for online video content and streaming platforms
options.C: The EU Digital Services Act and the existing E-Commerce Directive framework, which limit the liability of passive hosts who act expeditiously upon notification of illegal content
options.D: The EU Copyright Directive, which establishes the conditions for online platform liability in respect of user-generated content

questions.explanation: Platform liability for defamatory user-generated content must be considered alongside the EU Digital Services Act and the existing E-Commerce Directive framework, which limit the liability of passive hosts that do not have actual knowledge of illegal content and that act expeditiously upon receiving notification of such content. The Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024 engaged with platform liability in the context of this EU framework.
